intro:
Prayer is the believer’s direct address to God — communion of the soul with its Maker — encompassing adoration, thanksgiving, confession, intercession, and petition. It rests on the conviction that the living God is personal, near, and willing to hear: “The LORD is near to all who call on him, to all who call on him in truth” (Psalms 145:18). From earliest Scripture “people began to call upon the name of the LORD” (Genesis 4:26), and the whole Bible assumes that God both hears and answers.

The Old Testament is rich with prayer. Abraham interceded for Sodom (Genesis 18:23-33), Moses pleaded for rebellious Israel (Exodus 32:11-14), Hannah poured out her soul in silence (1 Samuel 1:13-15), and Solomon dedicated the temple as a house of prayer for all peoples (1 Kings 8:27-30). The Psalter is the inspired school of prayer, voicing praise, lament, repentance, and trust in every season. Daniel knelt toward Jerusalem three times a day even under sentence of death (Daniel 6:10), modelling steadfast devotion.

Jesus is the supreme teacher and example of prayer. He withdrew often to lonely places to pray (Luke 5:16), prayed through the night before choosing the Twelve, and wrestled in Gethsemane, “not my will, but yours, be done” (Luke 22:42). He gave his disciples the Lord’s Prayer as both pattern and gift (Matthew 6:9-13), warned against empty repetition and showy display (Matthew 6:5-7), and urged bold, persistent asking: “ask, and it will be given to you” (Matthew 7:7; Luke 18:1-8). He taught that prayer in his name, according to God’s will, is heard (John 14:13-14).

For the Church, prayer is the breath of the new life in the Spirit. Believers approach God as “Abba, Father” because the Spirit of the Son dwells in them, and that same Spirit “helps us in our weakness … intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words” (Romans 8:15; Romans 8:26) — while the risen Christ ever lives to intercede for them (Hebrews 7:25). Hence the apostolic call to “pray without ceasing” (1 Thessalonians 5:17) and to bring every anxiety to God “by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving,” receiving the peace that guards the heart (Philippians 4:6-7). The prayer of faith is powerful and effective (James 5:16), for it lays hold of a Father who delights to give good gifts to his children.
